concept: an adjacency object maps each node to its list of neighbors
cue: Look up node 0's neighbor list, then take the first neighbor of node 2.
note: A directed graph is stored as an object from each node to an array of the nodes it points to; `graph[node]` reads that array in a fixed order.

concept: each neighbor list's length is that node's out-degree
cue: Add up how many neighbors each node lists.
note: Object number keys stringify, so walk a known node list and read each `graph[node]` array; the array order is the order a traversal will follow.

concept: depth-first search goes as deep as it can before backing up
cue: Start at 0 and follow the first neighbor all the way down before trying the next one.
note: DFS visits 0, dives into 1 then 3, and only after that comes back for 2; following neighbors in array order makes the result order fixed.

cue: Mark a node visited and record it, then recurse into each unvisited neighbor in order.
prompt: Write `dfs(graph, start)` where `graph` is an adjacency object mapping each node to an array of its neighbors. Return an array of the nodes in the order depth-first search first reaches them, starting at `start`. Visit each node once, following each node's neighbor array in order.
note: Each node and each edge is looked at once, so this is O(V + E) time; the visited set and call stack add O(V) space.

concept: breadth-first search visits nearer nodes before farther ones
cue: Take nodes off the front of the queue and add their new neighbors to the back.
note: On the same graph DFS gave [0, 1, 3, 2]; BFS reads all of 0's neighbors first, so 2 comes before 3. The queue is what makes the order breadth-first.

cue: Keep a queue: mark a node visited when you add it, then read it later and enqueue its unvisited neighbors.
prompt: Write `bfs(graph, start)` where `graph` is an adjacency object mapping each node to an array of its neighbors. Return an array of the nodes in the order breadth-first search reaches them, starting at `start`. Use a queue and mark a node visited as you enqueue it; follow each node's neighbor array in order.
note: Every node is enqueued once and every edge is checked once, so O(V + E) time; the queue and visited set use O(V) space.

cue: Search out from the start; if you ever land on the target, it is reachable.
prompt: Write `canReach(graph, start, target)` where `graph` is an adjacency object mapping each node to an array of its neighbors. Return `true` if `target` can be reached by following edges from `start` (a node reaches itself), else `false`. Do not loop forever on a cycle.
note: This is a search that stops as soon as it finds the target; the visited set keeps a cycle from looping forever. O(V + E) time, O(V) space.

concept: a connected component is a group of nodes joined by edges
cue: Each undirected edge links two nodes; count how many separate groups the links form.
note: Nodes 0, 1, 2 form one group and 3, 4 form another, so there are two components. Each start of a fresh search adds one to the count.

cue: Build an undirected adjacency, then start one search per unvisited node and count the starts.
prompt: Write `countComponents(n, edges)` for an undirected graph on nodes `0` to `n - 1`, where `edges` is an array of `[u, v]` pairs. Return the number of connected components. Build an adjacency object first (each edge joins both directions), then search from each unvisited node.
note: Every node and edge is visited once across all the searches, so O(V + E) time and O(V + E) space for the adjacency and visited set.

concept: a grid cell has four neighbors from four offsets
cue: Add each offset to the cell's row and column to get an adjacent cell.
note: The four offsets (-1,0), (1,0), (0,-1), (0,1) are up, down, left, right; a grid is a graph whose edges are these four steps to neighbor cells.

cue: Scan every cell; on each unseen `1`, add one and sink its whole island by flipping connected `1`s to `0`.
prompt: Write `numIslands(grid)` where `grid` is a 2D array of `0`s (water) and `1`s (land). Return the number of islands, where an island is a group of `1`s connected up, down, left, or right. You may change `grid`. Scan the cells in row then column order.
note: Each cell is visited a constant number of times, so O(rows * cols) time; the recursion can go O(rows * cols) deep in the worst case.

concept: flood fill spreads to same-colored neighbors
cue: Repaint the start cell and every same-colored cell connected to it; cells of other colors stop the spread.
note: Only `1`s joined to the start by up/down/left/right steps turn into `2`; the lone `1` in the corner is cut off by `0`s, so it stays.

cue: Remember the start color, then paint the start cell and spread only to neighbors that still hold that old color.
prompt: Write `floodFill(image, sr, sc, color)` where `image` is a 2D array of colors. Repaint the cell at row `sr`, column `sc` and every cell connected to it up, down, left, or right that shares its original color, then return the modified `image`. If the start cell already has `color`, return `image` unchanged.
note: The check `start === color` avoids looping forever when the fill color already matches; otherwise each cell is painted once, O(rows * cols) time and up to O(rows * cols) stack depth.
